fact_text: The reported system size was 108,000 lines of C# code.
The proposed infrastructure has three components: a hot-memory constitution, 19 specialized agents, and a cold-memory knowledge base of 34 documents.
The architecture assigns different loading strategies to each tier: constitution always loaded, specialist agents invoked per task, and knowledge-base documents retrieved on demand.
The constitution uses trigger tables to route tasks to specialized agents based mainly on which files are being modified.
The paper states that over half of each project agent specification was project-domain knowledge rather than behavioral instructions.
The knowledge base was served through an MCP server that exposed five search tools, including find_relevant_context(task) and suggest_agent(task).
The current retrieval implementation used keyword substring matching rather than semantic retrieval.
The project was developed across 283 sessions over 70 days of part-time work and 148 commits.
The final context infrastructure totaled about 54 files and 26,200 lines, equal to 24.2% of code size.
The author states that the 24.2% knowledge-to-code ratio should not be treated as a target or general finding.
The interaction dataset included 2,801 human prompts, 1,197 agent invocations, and 16,522 autonomous agent turns.
About 13% of sessions used a structured plan-execute-review workflow, while about 87% were ad hoc.
In the save-system case study, the specification was referenced in 74 sessions and 12 agent conversations, and the paper reports no save-related bugs across five later persistence features.
In the deterministic RNG debugging case, replacing a time-bucket hash input with a synchronized shot counter resolved the desynchronization bug.
Specification staleness was reported as the primary failure mode, and the evaluation was limited to a single developer on a single project without controlled experiments.
